evidence_text: The study analyzed 2,303 agent context files from 1,925 repositories.
source_excerpt: “We conduct the first large-scale empirical study of 2,303 agent context files from 1,925 repositories.”
note: The abstract frames this as the first large-scale empirical study.
caveat: The abstract frames this as the first large-scale empirical study.

evidence_text: The dataset included 922 Claude Code files, 694 OpenAI Codex files, and 687 GitHub Copilot files.
source_excerpt: “In total, we collect 922 Claude Code files, 694 OpenAI Codex files, and 687 GitHub Copilot files across 1,925 repositories.”
note: These counts are tied to filename-based retrieval using each tool’s documented naming convention.
caveat: These counts are tied to filename-based retrieval using each tool’s documented naming convention.

evidence_text: The repository sample started from 8,370 AIDev repositories with at least 5 GitHub stars.
source_excerpt: “From this dataset, we select 8,370 repositories with at least 5 GitHub stars to exclude toy projects.”
note: This is a sampling constraint on the study corpus, not the full population of agent-using repositories.
caveat: This is a sampling constraint on the study corpus, not the full population of agent-using repositories.

evidence_text: GitHub Copilot context files had a median length of 535.0 words, Claude Code 485.0 words, and OpenAI Codex 335.5 words.
source_excerpt: “Context files for GitHub Copilot (median 535.0 words) and Claude Code (median 485.0 words) are similarly extensive... both are significantly longer than OpenAI Codex context files (median 335.5 words).”
note: The source states no statistically significant length difference between GitHub Copilot and Claude Code, but both exceed OpenAI Codex.
caveat: The source states no statistically significant length difference between GitHub Copilot and Claude Code, but both exceed OpenAI Codex.

evidence_text: Median Flesch Reading Ease scores were 16.6 for Claude Code files, 26.6 for GitHub Copilot files, and 39.6 for OpenAI Codex files.
source_excerpt: “Claude Code context files are the most difficult to read, with a median FRE score of 16.6... GitHub Copilot context files, while also ‘very difficult’ (median FRE 26.6)... OpenAI Codex context files are the easiest to read of the group, with a median FRE score of 39.6 (‘difficult’).”
note: This evidence is mechanism-bounded because it documents readability difficulty rather than directly explaining how file content produces downstream agent behavior.
caveat: This evidence is mechanism-bounded because it documents readability difficulty rather than directly explaining how file content produces downstream agent behavior.

evidence_text: Agent context files typically use one H1 heading, around 6 to 7 H2 headings, and deeper headings are rare.
source_excerpt: “The structure is almost always a single, top-level H1 heading, with a median count of 1.0 for all context files... the context files branch into a moderate number of H2 headings (median 6 to 7)... Deeply nested structures (H5 and beyond) are extremely rare.”
note: The source also reports that H5 headers appear in only 6 Claude Code files and H6 headers are non-existent.
caveat: The source also reports that H5 headers appear in only 6 Claude Code files and H6 headers are non-existent.

evidence_text: The maintenance analysis covered 5,655 Claude Code commits, 2,767 OpenAI Codex commits, and 2,237 GitHub Copilot commits that added, deleted, or modified context files.
source_excerpt: “This yields 5,655 commits for Claude Code, 2,767 commits for OpenAI Codex, and 2,237 commits for GitHub Copilot.”
note: These are commits touching context files, not all repository commits.
caveat: These are commits touching context files, not all repository commits.

evidence_text: A majority of context files were modified in multiple commits: 67.4% for Claude Code, 59.7% for GitHub Copilot, and 59.2% for OpenAI Codex.
source_excerpt: “A majority of Claude Code context files (67.4%) are modified in multiple commits... This pattern also holds, though slightly less frequent, for GitHub Copilot (59.7%) and OpenAI Codex (59.2%).”
note: The source presents this as evidence that these files are routinely revisited rather than written once.
caveat: The source presents this as evidence that these files are routinely revisited rather than written once.

evidence_text: Median intervals between context-file updates were 24.1 hours for Claude Code, 22.0 hours for OpenAI Codex, and 70.7 hours for GitHub Copilot.
source_excerpt: “Figure 5(b) shows that the median interval is 70.7 hours (around 3 days) for GitHub Copilot, 24.1 hours (around 1 day) for Claude Code, and 22.0 hours (around 1 day) for OpenAI Codex.”
note: The source states these interval distributions differ significantly across all compared pairs with p < 0.001.
caveat: The source states these interval distributions differ significantly across all compared pairs with p < 0.001.

evidence_text: Context-file evolution is dominated by additions, while deletions remain small; deletions have median values below 15.0 words, and Claude Code commits add a median of 57.0 words.
source_excerpt: “Deletions are consistently negligible across all context file types, with median values less than 15.0 words. In contrast, additions are more substantial and variable. Claude Code updates tend to be the most substantial, with a median of 57.0 words added per commit.”
note: The source says Claude Code additions exceed OpenAI Codex and GitHub Copilot, although the effect size is negligible.
caveat: The source says Claude Code additions exceed OpenAI Codex and GitHub Copilot, although the effect size is negligible.

evidence_text: The manual content analysis produced 16 final instruction labels from 332 Claude Code files and 2,069 final label assignments after disagreement resolution.
source_excerpt:
“This process resulted in 2,227 total label assignments across the 332 files, with 438 instances of disagreement (80.3% of agreement rate)... This conciliation process results in a total of 2,069 labels.”
“We identified 16 distinct labels...”
note: The source also states all three inspectors had 4 to 17 years of programming experience.
caveat: The source also states all three inspectors had 4 to 17 years of programming experience.

evidence_text: The most prevalent instruction categories were Testing (75.0%), Implementation Details (69.9%), Architecture (67.7%), Development Process (63.3%), and Build and Run (62.3%).
source_excerpt: “Testing” appears at “75.0”; “Impl. Details” at “69.9”; “Architecture” at “67.7”; “Development Process” at “63.3”; and “Build and Run” at “62.3” in Table 3.
note: These prevalence rates are reported for the manually analyzed Claude Code subset.
caveat: These prevalence rates are reported for the manually analyzed Claude Code subset.

evidence_text: Non-functional categories were uncommon in the manual analysis, with Security at 14.5%, Performance at 14.5%, and UI/UX at 8.7%; automatic classification reached a micro-average F1-score of 0.79 and performed worse on abstract categories such as AI Integration (0.48 F1) and Project Management (0.42 F1).
citation: Chatlatanagulchai et al. 2025, Table 3 and Table 4
source_excerpt: “We conduct the first large-scale empirical study of 2,303 agent context files from 1,925 repositories.”
note: The abstract frames this as the first large-scale empirical study.
caveat: The abstract frames this as the first large-scale empirical study.
